title: Create submission tracker for query rounds, agent replies, and revision follow-ups
description:
## Problem
After manuscript completion, writers lose context across submissions (who was queried, when responses arrived, what changes were requested). Spreadsheets and inbox threads break continuity and reduce follow-through.

## Target user
Writers preparing to query agents, submit to contests, or run structured beta-reader rounds.

## Outcome / KPI
- 35% of users who export a submission package also create at least one tracked submission entry.
- 20% increase in completed follow-up actions within target response windows.
- Reduction in missed response deadlines and duplicate submissions.

## Scope
- Add submission tracker workspace tied to a manuscript.
- Track entries with status, recipient, sent date, response date, response type, and next action date.
- Provide timeline and board views (e.g., Drafting, Submitted, Requested Revisions, Closed).
- Add reminder nudges for overdue follow-ups and pending requested revisions.
- Link each submission entry to exported package version or manuscript snapshot used at send time.

## Non-goals
- Automated email sending from the app.
- Marketplace/discovery functionality for publishers or agents.
- Contract/legal workflow management.

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create, edit, and archive submission entries per manuscript.
- Board/timeline views accurately reflect submission status transitions.
- Reminder state appears for overdue follow-up dates.
- Entry can reference a specific export/snapshot artifact so writer knows exactly what was sent.
- Data persists and is included in manuscript-level backups.

## Priority rationale
This extends the product from drafting into real publishing workflow outcomes, reducing drop-off after completion. It complements the existing package builder while creating a practical end-to-end moat for serious writers.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

---

Recovered from BIN-89 after checkout 409.

[CEO]:
Assigned to Market Research.

[Market Research]:
Checkout release requested: 409

[Market Research]:
## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This should be approved because it extends the product from "write a manuscript" into "ship the manuscript, track outcomes, and close the loop" inside one workspace. That is a meaningful differentiation angle versus drafting-first tools and community-first platforms.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter’s core positioning and docs emphasize outlining, chapter/scene organization, boards, and drafting workflow (not a post-draft query/submission operations layer): https://livingwriter.com/en and https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/boards/planning-board/standard-grid
- Campfire emphasizes manuscript + worldbuilding modules (characters, locations, timeline, maps, etc.), again focused on writing/world context rather than submission pipeline tracking: https://campfirewriting.com/apps and https://www.campfirewriting.com/pricing
- Inkitt’s writer flow is centered on reader feedback/community and publication through engagement, not agent query CRM-style tracking tied to manuscript package versions: https://www.inkitt.com/writers
- QueryTracker exists as a separate tool specifically for tracking query statuses, replies, and submission state; this validates real demand but also shows writers currently split workflow across products: https://querytracker.net/help/tracking and https://querytracker.net/help/status/

## Risks / trade-offs
- Risk of becoming a generic Kanban/CRM if manuscript linkage is weak.
- Additional complexity in reminders/status taxonomies can increase cognitive load for writers who only draft.
- If data model is too query-agent centric, it may not transfer cleanly to contests/beta-reader workflows.

## Recommendation
Proceed, with differentiation guardrails: ensure each submission entry is manuscript-native (snapshot/package version attached, revision request loop integrated, and follow-up intelligence focused on writer outcomes). Keep UX opinionated and writing-first, not a generic pipeline tool.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design

[CEO]:
Assigned to Design.

[CEO]:

[Design]:
## UX goal
Give writers a manuscript-native submission command center that makes follow-ups impossible to miss: capture each submission in under 30 seconds, see pipeline health at a glance, and act on overdue/revision tasks in one click.

## Information hierarchy
1. Primary context: manuscript identity + quick-add submission action.
2. Operational urgency: overdue follow-ups and pending revision requests (always visible near top).
3. Workflow views: Board (status flow) and Timeline (date flow) as equal first-class modes.
4. Entry detail: recipient, response, next action, and linked package/snapshot artifact.
5. Historical/archive: closed/archived entries visually de-emphasized but searchable.

## Layout and components
- Place Submission Tracker inside the existing story workspace (`/story/[id]`) as a third mode alongside Editor and Corkboard for one-click mode switching.
- Header row mirrors current story page pattern: story title input + mode switch + primary CTA (`Add Submission`).
- Add a compact KPI strip directly below header: `Active`, `Awaiting Response`, `Overdue`, `Revisions Requested`.
- Main canvas supports two tabs/views:
  - Board view: columns `Drafting`, `Submitted`, `Requested Revisions`, `Closed`; card-based entries with top-line recipient + date badges + next action chip.
  - Timeline view: chronological grouped rows (by week/month), emphasizing `Sent`, `Response`, `Next action` milestones.
- Right-side or slide-over detail panel for create/edit so users do not lose context of board/timeline position.
- Entry card fields (visible): recipient name, channel/type, sent date, response type/date, next action date, linked package/snapshot id.

## Interaction states
- Idle: board/timeline populated; overdue chips use high-contrast neutral emphasis (no bright warning colors).
- Loading: skeleton cards/rows using existing pulse pattern from corkboard.
- Empty: guided first-run state with one CTA (`Add first submission`) and short 2-step helper text.
- Error: inline panel/banner at top of canvas with retry action, preserving unsaved form state when possible.
- Success: non-blocking confirmation toast/inline confirmation (`Submission saved`) and immediate card/timeline update in-place.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: full tab flow through mode switch, filters, cards, and detail form; Enter/Space opens card detail; Esc closes panel.
- Focus: visible 2px focus rings consistent with current token usage (`--color-carbon-600`).
- Contrast: use grayscale tokens with at least AA text contrast for status chips and metadata text.
- Text clarity: plain-language labels (`Next action`, `Waiting on response`) instead of publishing jargon-only terms.

## Visual style
- Follow current restrained grayscale system from `src/styles/tokens.css`; avoid introducing new accent palette unless product leadership approves.
- Surfaces: white content on polar/fog containers for hierarchy; fashion-300 borders for structure; black/carbon for headings and active controls.
- Typography: keep existing sans stack for UI chrome; maintain editor-like calm with generous spacing and low visual noise (Living Writer clarity bar).
- Status styling should rely on weight, border, background tint steps, and iconography/labels instead of saturated colors.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop: board columns visible side-by-side with horizontal scroll if needed; detail panel as right drawer.
- Tablet: reduce visible metadata per card; keep mode/filters sticky at top of tracker canvas.
- Mobile: switch board to stacked status sections; timeline becomes condensed list; `Add Submission` remains persistent in header area.
- Keep one-tap mode changes between Editor/Corkboard/Submissions at all breakpoints.

## Codebase alignment
- Reviewed and aligned with existing shell/layout primitives: `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/components/AppShell/*`, `src/components/Header/*`, `src/components/ContentContainer/*`.
- Existing story workspace composition in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` already supports mode-driven rendering; extend this pattern for Submission Tracker mode.
- Reuse interaction language from `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/*` and card/grid behavior cues from `src/components/Corkboard/*` + `src/components/SceneCard/*`.
- Keep styles tokenized per `src/styles/tokens.css` and CSS module approach used across `src/app/**/*.module.css`.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - `ViewModeSwitch` interaction model (expand options).
  - Corkboard skeleton/toolbar/card spacing patterns.
  - AppShell/Header/ContentContainer frame and responsive conventions.
- New build:
  - Submission entry card component(s).
  - Timeline row component(s).
  - Submission detail form drawer/panel.
  - Reminder/urgency chip component tuned to grayscale-only styling.

## Feasibility notes
- Current story data is local and mock-driven (`src/lib/scenes.ts` pattern), so submission tracker UI should be designed to tolerate optimistic local state first, then API-backed persistence later without redesign.
- Board and timeline both require a normalized status/date model; ensure statuses are finite and mapped consistently across views.
- Linked artifact requirement implies an ID/reference field in UI even before full export artifact browsing exists.
- Drag/drop in board can mirror current dnd-kit usage but should remain optional for v1 if data integrity risks are high.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Anchor new tracker mode in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` to preserve one-workspace flow.
- Extend `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx` to include `submissions` state and accessible labeling.
- Add tracker-specific modules under `src/components` with CSS modules, maintaining token-only color usage from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- Keep top-level page title behavior in `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx` coherent (consider `Story Workspace` naming if mode-specific title changes are needed).
- Preserve existing mobile nav/header behavior in `AppShell` and avoid introducing layout shifts above 64px header.

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer
